text: The surgeon carefully dissects the pelvic sidewall structures, successfully resecting the obturator lymph nodes using a laparoscopic approach.

The laparoscopic view shows the resection of a large obturator lymph node in a rectal cancer patient, with the rectum still in situ. The surgeon performs dissection within the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, carefully navigating the anatomical structures.

In the left pelvic sidewall, a large metastatic lymph node is clearly visible. The surgeon opens medially from the ureter to access the lymph node, aiming to minimize dissection in the parorectal space and prevent damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon opens the parorectal space due to the size and location of the lymph node, exposing the anatomical structures. Dissection is performed lateral to the ureter to enhance visualization of the parorectal structures.
The video provides an overview of the parorectal structures, with a section parallel to the external iliac artery that safely expands the parorectal space.
The surgeon identifies the external iliac vessels, noting the absence of pelvic branches, while the inferior epigastric vessels become visible as the dissection progresses distally.

The surgeon identifies a branch from the external iliac vein, highlighting anatomical variance. This vein branching appears aberrant or accessory, forming a confluence with the superior physical vein and the obturator vein, which typically drains into the internal iliac vein.
The surgeon carefully dissects the pelvic sidewall to mobilize an enlarged lymph node, meticulously separating it from the adjacent obturator nerve.

The surgeon achieves mobility of the malignant lymph node, providing a clear view of the parorectal space and its anatomical structures. The lymph node is then placed into an extraction bag for removal with the rectal specimen.
Following the lymph node dissection, the surgeon completes the rectal resection laparoscopically, using minimally invasive techniques to remove the rectal specimen.
